title: The Lord Who Fled the Crown
subtitle: Christ slips into the mountain alone, escaping the hands that would crown him

stillness: Be still, and let the noise of ambition fall silent in you.
Before you reach for any height, sit down low.
Consider quietly how the Lord truly, and not in pretense, fled honor.
gospel: He sent his disciples across the sea,
and did not enter the boat with them,
lest the crowds should seek him among his disciples.
Then, while they were not watching,
behold: See with how great care and caution he fled and turned away from honor.
See: he did not pretend, as those do who, lest they seem low before men, refuse to ask for what their heart desires.
He gave us an example, that we too should do the same.
For he fled not for his own sake, but for ours.
consider: There are many, and chiefly the religious, who pretend not to seek high office;
yet when it is offered, they run to it with hands and feet, and receive it gladly — even, at times, offer themselves of their own accord.
There are some among them who, once placed in dignities, feign that they wish not to hold them;
yet by themselves and by go-betweens they scheme, and anxiously seek occasions to remain in those offices.
No such pretense and feigning was in the Lord.
For he knew how great a rashness it is, if we should reach after honors.
Honor is among the greatest snares, and the heavier weights, for the overthrow of souls.
Scarcely can it happen that one who delights in honor is not in danger or on the brink — or, what is worse, already dashed to pieces by the fall.
Ambition is the worst of vices, the cause and the root of many other vices.
And what does it profit them, if they gain the whole world, but lose themselves, and suffer the ruin of their own soul?
Today, commonly, honor is looked to and not the burden, glory and not the penalty.
Men blush to be a plain cleric in the Church, and reckon themselves vile and inglorious if they are not lifted to some higher place.
Climb, then, since the heaviest judgment is made upon those who preside, and the powerful shall be powerfully tormented.
Where are you going, O wretch? That from a higher rung the fall may be the heavier?
For you will not sink down little by little; but like lightning, with a violent rush, like a second Satan, you will suddenly be cast down.
We rejoice at the ascent; let us fear the fall.
The heart of the ambitious never rests: either it craves the honor it does not have, or it dreads to lose the one it has;
and so the devil moves and turns his heart forever, like a mill that never stops.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, you who resist the proud and give grace to the humble,
grant to all who take up the state of dignity and rule for the good of obedience, humbly and without ambition, and so persevere in it,
grace in the present, and glory in time to come;
and resist the ambitious, hedging their ways with thorns, that they may not prosper in them,
so that they neither delight to preside over men, nor rejoice in the singularity of their honor,
and that at last, enlightened and returned to their heart, they may desist from the deadly vice of ambition,
and cease to be such, that — by your mercy — they may not perish forever. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Cor enim ambitiosi numquam quiescit; sed vel honorem, quem non habet appetit, vel eum quem habet amittere metuit.
latinJewel.english: For the heart of the ambitious never rests: either it craves the honor it does not have, or it dreads to lose the one it has.
